original_abstract: Until recently, we knew very little about the role of populist governments in EU decision-making. The “crucial case” of refugee distribution within the EU demonstrated that their behaviour was ruled by unpolitics: they rejected formal and informal rules of decision-making if these were not conducive to their preferred outcome, they rejected traditional means of ensuring compromises, and they rejected solutions to perpetuate crises. However, to what extent is unpolitics a phenomenon unique to migration—an area prone to (nativist) populist capture? This thematic issue compares the behaviour of populist governments in the Council of the EU across different policy areas. The goal is to better understand under which conditions unpolitics is more likely to manifest in EU decision-making. We argue that unpolitics is intrinsically linked to vote-seeking strategies, where populist governments use EU decision-making to mobilise domestic audiences. Hence, unpolitics is more prone to “high gain” and “low risk” issues, since they can be more easily politicised. Unpolitics is also more likely to manifest in venues that act as a tribune, where populist actors can directly speak to domestic audiences. Finally, since unpolitics relies on the mobilisation of voters, it is essentially a two-level game largely determined by domestic political and socioeconomic conditions. Overall, we see that, although the EU institutions have proved relatively resilient, unpolitics is gradually unsettling and hollowing out norms, institutions, and discourses.
humanized_abstract: There was a time when we did not know very much about the way populist governments would behave in the Council of the EU. The “crucial case” of refugee distribution across the EU has provided the initial example of the practice of what we call “unpolitics”, referring to the way populist leaders engage in EU decision-making when their policy preferences are not respected through established rules of procedure (formal and informal), when they refuse to agree to compromises and procedures that prevent policy deadlocks from arising, and when they prevent the search for a solution to such deadlocks from taking place. However, the way populist leaders behave in the Council will depend on the policy area and it remains to be seen whether migration policy is an exception due to its particular nativist tone. The purpose of this thematic issue is to provide a first assessment of populist leadership in the Council of the EU in different policy areas and thereby better understand the conditions under which unpolitics is observed. We will then be able to say more about what unpolitics is. A first hypothesis that will be discussed is that unpolitics is vote-seeking behaviour and that populist leaders seek to maximise this behaviour by opting for “high gain”, “low risk” issues that are highly politicisable. In addition, we will also argue that unpolitics is more likely to take place in those instances in which EU councils act as a “tribune”, that is, when there is a direct relation between the European representatives and the domestic public to whom they have to report. Ultimately, unpolitics will remain a two-level game in which domestic politics and socio-economic conditions will condition and determine the ways in which European policies are shaped. And our concern will be to observe how these ways of behaving progressively erode and hollow out the norms, institutions and discourse of the EU.
